message.content: can you list the directorys that are part of this project pelase

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

## plugin:github:github
The GitHub MCP Server provides tools to interact with GitHub platform.

Tool selection guidance:
	1. Use 'list_*' tools for broad, simple retrieval and pagination of all items of a type (e.g., all issues, all PRs, all branches) with basic filtering.
	2. Use 'search_*' tools for targeted queries with specific criteria, keywords, or complex filters (e.g., issues with certain text, PRs by author, code containing functions).

Context management:
	1. Use pagination whenever possible with batches of 5-10 items.
	2. Use minimal_output parameter set to true if the full information is not needed to accomplish a task.

Tool usage guidance:
	1. For 'search_*' tools: Use separate 'sort' and 'order' parameters if available for sorting results - do not include 'sort:' syntax in query strings. Query strings should contain only search criteria (e.g., 'org:google language:python'), not sorting instructions. Always call 'get_me' first to understand current user permissions and context. ## Issues

Check 'list_issue_types' first for organizations to use proper issue types. Use 'search_issues' before creating new issues to avoid duplicates. Always set 'state_reason' when closing issues. ## Pull Requests

PR review workflow: Always use 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'create' to create a pending review, then 'add_comment_to_pending_review' to add comments, and finally 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'submit_pending' to submit the review for complex reviews with line-specific comments.

Before creating a pull request, search for pull request templates in the repository. Template files are called pull_request_template.md or they're located in '.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE' directory. Use the template content to structure the PR description and then call create_pull_request tool.

attachment.content:
- defuddle: Extract clean markdown content from web pages using Defuddle CLI, removing clutter and navigation to save tokens. Use instead of WebFetch when the user provides a URL to read or analyze, for online documentation, articles, blog posts, or any standard web page. Do NOT use for URLs ending in .md — those are already markdown, use WebFetch directly.
- obsidian-bases: Create and edit Obsidian Bases (.base files) with views, filters, formulas, and summaries. Use when working with .base files, creating database-like views of notes, or when the user mentions Bases, table views, card views, filters, or formulas in Obsidian.
- obsidian-cli: Interact with Obsidian vaults using the Obsidian CLI to read, create, search, and manage notes, tasks, properties, and more. Also supports plugin and theme development with commands to reload plugins, run JavaScript, capture errors, take screenshots, and inspect the DOM. Use when the user asks to interact with their Obsidian vault, manage notes, search vault content, perform vault operations from the command line, or develop and debug Obsidian plugins and themes.
- obsidian-markdown: Create and edit Obsidian Flavored Markdown with wikilinks, embeds, callouts, properties, and other Obsidian-specific syntax. Use when working with .md files in Obsidian, or when the user mentions wikilinks, callouts, frontmatter, tags, embeds, or Obsidian notes.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- claude-md-management:revise-claude-md: Update CLAUDE.md with learnings from this session
- claude-md-management:claude-md-improver: Audit and improve CLAUDE.md files in repositories. Use when user asks to check, audit, update, improve, or fix CLAUDE.md files. Scans for all CLAUDE.md files, evaluates quality against templates, outputs quality report, then makes targeted updates. Also use when the user mentions "CLAUDE.md maintenance" or "project memory optimization".
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Now the signature is unambiguous: even `xattr -l` on the directory fails with EPERM, and `ls -ld` on it (reading the entry from the parent) *works* while reading its **contents** fails. Normal Unix perms, no ACLs, no BSD lock flags, local disk. `opendir()` returning EPERM like this is what a **sandbox deny** looks like — and notably, Claude Code's Bash tool runs inside a macOS sandbox (that's why the tool even has a `dangerouslyDisableSandbox` option). Your *primary* working dir is under `~/Documents` too and works fine, so this isn't a blanket TCC block — it's that this specific path isn't in the sandbox's allowlist.

Let me confirm by running the exact same command with the sandbox disabled:

input.description: List target dir with sandbox disabled

content.content:
ls: /Users/****/Documents/mp-docs-synced/workflow-tools/family-cookbook: Operation not permitted
total 0

content.text: Even with the sandbox off it's still EPERM — so this is **not** the Claude Code sandbox. It's the OS denying the host process itself. Let me map exactly where the denial boundary is, which tells us whether it's process-level TCC or something specific to the `mp-docs-synced` subtree:

input.command:
for p in \
  /Users/****/Documents \
  /Users/****/Documents/working_folders \
  /Users/****/Documents/mp-docs-synced \
  /Users/****/Documents/mp-docs-synced/workflow-tools ; do
  printf '%-70s ' "$p:"
  if ls "$p" >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo "OK (readable)"; else echo "DENIED (EPERM/EACCES)"; fi
done
echo ""
echo "=== ~/Library/CloudStorage (file-provider sync roots) ==="
ls -la /Users/****/Library/CloudStorage 2>&1 | head
echo ""
echo "=== Real resolved path of mp-docs-synced ==="
python3 -c "import os; print(os.path.realpath('/Users/****/Documents/mp-docs-synced'))" 2>&1

1	# Requirements: Family Meal Planning Workflow
2	
3	Derived from [[Project Plan]]. This document captures the MVP requirements for a
4	Claude-powered weekly dinner-planning workflow for a household, designed to be
5	backed up on GitHub and shared with other families.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## 1. Purpose & Problem
10	
11	Family meal planning is a burdensome weekly task with many inputs and
12	dependencies that affects daily happiness. Done well it improves day-to-day life
13	with compounding effects on health and budget; done poorly it adds stress at the
14	end of a long day. Today the process is inconsistent week to week, rushed, and
15	cascades into either food waste (few shared ingredients) or overspending (one
16	convenient store).
17	
18	**Goal:** a repeatable workflow that plans the week's dinners, produces a
19	store-grouped grocery list, and schedules prep reminders — so the family sticks
20	to dietary goals, shops smarter, and wastes less food.
21	
22	## 2. Users
23	
24	- **Primary:** the two partners in the household who alternate meal-planning duty
25	  each week.
26	- **Secondary:** other families who clone the shared GitHub project and run it
27	  for their own household after completing kickoff.
28	
29	## 3. Success Criteria
30	
31	- A full week of needed dinners is planned in a single sitting (no mid-week
32	  "rush and order out").
33	- Weekly planning time drops meaningfully below the current ~1 hr + ~15 min/day.
34	- Grocery list is organized so shopping is efficient and cost-aware.
35	- Prep reminders remove the daily "did we thaw the beef?" scramble.
36	- Another family can clone the repo, complete kickoff, and run their first week.
37	
38	---
39	
40	## 4. Scope
41	
42	### 4.1 In Scope (MVP)
43	
44	- One-time **kickoff** to capture household config.
45	- **Weekly run** (scheduled + manual) that plans dinners, builds the grocery
46	  list, and schedules prep reminders.
47	- **Hybrid recipe sourcing**: personal collection first, AI/web suggestions to
48	  fill gaps.
49	- **Store-grouped grocery list** using simple ranked-store logic.
50	- **Ratings** feedback loop collected twice weekly.
51	- **Shareable** project structure (templates + gitignored personal data).
52	
53	### 4.2 Out of Scope (deferred to later versions)
54	
55	- **Full grocery price optimization** — splitting a shopping trip across multiple
56	  stores based on live sale/circular pricing to minimize spend. MVP uses only
57	  simple ranked-store grouping (see 5.4). True price optimization requires sale
58	  data that stores do not expose via API.
59	- Breakfast/lunch planning (dinners only for MVP).
60	- Automated ordering / grocery delivery integration.
61	- Nutrition/macro tracking.
62	
63	---
64	
65	## 5. Functional Requirements
66	
67	### 5.1 Kickoff (one-time per household)
68	
69	Captured once and stored as editable config files (see 7). Collect:
70	
71	1. Home address (used to derive nearby grocery stores).
72	2. Ranked list of grocery stores the family shops at.
73	3. Ideal/target dinner prep time.
74	4. Weekly budget.
75	5. Household members (names, ages).
76	6. Per-individual dietary preferences and requirements, including ingredients to
77	   avoid per person.
78	7. Ingredients typically on hand (spices, canned items, oils — pantry staples).
79	8. Cooking equipment available (stovetop, oven, air fryer, crockpot, etc.).
80	9. **Portion strategy** — how to size cooking when members are out on different
81	   nights (e.g. scale to who's home, plan deliberate leftovers, always
82	   full-family). This is a per-family preference, not fixed logic.
83	10. **Meal-planner run timing** — the family's preferred day/time for the weekly
84	    planning job (per-family; see 5.8).
85	11. Examples of up to 5 favorite dinner recipes (optional; seeds the collection).
86	
87	### 5.2 Weekly Run
88	
89	Triggered by the scheduled **meal-planner** job at the family's configured
90	day/time (set at kickoff; see 5.8), and also runnable **manually** at any time.
91	The job **pre-drafts** a plan and waits for the family's review/approval before
92	finalizing. Steps:
93	
94	1. **Read the calendar first.** Pull the week's schedule from Apple Calendar to
95	   detect dinners-out (work dinners, dinner at a friend's, travel) and determine
96	   how many dinners are needed, and for whom, each night.
97	2. Confirm/adjust the number of dinners to plan for the week (configurable).
98	3. Select dinners using **hybrid sourcing** (5.3), respecting dietary
99	   requirements, prep-time target, budget, and available equipment.
100	4. Favor recipes with **shared ingredients** to reduce waste.
101	5. Assign meals to days based on who is home and prep constraints.
102	6. Generate the **store-grouped grocery list** (5.4), excluding pantry staples
103	   already on hand.
104	7. Schedule **prep reminders** (5.6).
105	8. Deliver outputs (5.5).
106	
107	### 5.3 Recipe Sourcing (Hybrid)
108	
109	- Draw first from the family's **personal recipe collection**.
110	- Fill remaining slots with **AI-generated and/or web-sourced** suggestions
111	  matched to the family's preferences and ratings (5.7).
112	- **Collection growth:** the user pastes a recipe link (NYT, TikTok, Instagram,
113	  blog) or a photo of a recipe card, and Claude extracts it into a structured
114	  markdown file in the project's recipe collection.
115	
116	### 5.4 Grocery List (MVP)
117	
118	- Aggregate all needed ingredients across the week's recipes.
119	- Exclude items already on hand (pantry staples from kickoff).
120	- **Group the list by store** using simple ranked-store logic — e.g. if multiple
121	  recipes need chicken, assign chicken to the preferred bulk store (Costco).
122	- No live pricing/sale lookups in MVP (see Out of Scope).
123	
124	### 5.5 Outputs
125	
126	1. **Menu for the week, by day.**
127	2. **Recipes + step-by-step instructions.**
128	3. **Grocery list grouped by store**, delivered to **Apple Notes** (shared, so
129	   both partners can access; can be uploaded back into Claude for the guided
130	   cooking UX with timers).
131	4. **Prep reminders** in Apple Reminders (5.6).
132	
133	### 5.6 Prep Reminders
134	
135	- Written to a dedicated Apple Reminders list named **"Family Meals"**.
136	- **Per-recipe timing** based on what each recipe needs (e.g. defrost = night
137	  before, marinate = morning of).
138	
139	### 5.7 Ratings Feedback Loop
140	
141	- **Scale:** 1–5, with optional free-text notes (e.g. "loved the bowl format,"
142	  "too spicy," "halve the garlic next time"). The notes carry the real signal.
143	- **One rating per recipe** — the current rating, overwritten when re-rated —
144	  stored in the recipe's own markdown file (frontmatter), so the rating travels
145	  with the recipe and stays personal-but-shareable.
146	- **Captured by the recipe-reviewer job** (5.8), which prompts the family to
147	  rate recently cooked meals.
148	- **Usage in planning:**
149	  - Boost highly-rated recipes for re-suggestion.
150	  - Suppress low-rated recipes.
151	  - Use ratings **and notes** to find *similar* highly-rated recipes — the notes
152	    reveal the pattern the family likes. (Example: a note that they loved the
153	    "in a bowl" format of a taco bowl signals suggesting more "X in a bowl"
154	    meals, not just that specific taco recipe.)
155	
156	### 5.8 Scheduled Automation
157	
158	Two **independent** scheduled jobs, kept separate so feedback is collected more
159	frequently and feeds the next week's planning. Both notify via **push
160	notification**.
161	
162	1. **Recipe reviewer** — runs **twice weekly, Wednesday and Friday evenings**.
163	   Prompts the family to rate recently cooked meals (5.7). The higher review
164	   cadence keeps ratings fresh for the next planning run.
165	2. **Meal planner** — runs at the family's **preferred day/time (set at kickoff;
166	   5.1)**. **Pre-drafts** the week's plan from the calendar and preferences, then
167	   waits for the family's review/approval before finalizing. Also runnable
168	   manually (5.2).
169	
170	---
171	
172	## 6. Integrations & Platform
173	
174	- **Claude Desktop** — build and run platform; user-facing interface. Claude's
175	  mobile app provides the guided recipe/cooking UX (timers) when a recipe note
176	  is uploaded.
177	- **apple-events MCP** (`FradSer/mcp-server-apple-events`, installed
178	  project-scoped via `.mcp.json`) — reads **Apple Calendar** (schedule) and
179	  writes **Apple Reminders** (the "Family Meals" prep list).
180	  - Note: this changes the original plan's "Google Calendar" — schedule lives in
181	    Apple Calendar (confirmed).
182	- **Apple Notes** — destination for the weekly grocery list and recipe handoff to
183	  mobile.
184	- **GitHub** — the workflow is backed up as a shareable project so other
185	  households can clone and run it.
186	
187	---
188	
189	## 7. Data & Storage Model
190	
191	All persistent config and state live as **markdown files in the project** so they
192	can be edited directly or by chatting with Claude, and version-controlled.
193	
194	- **Template files** (committed, shareable): `*.example.md` with placeholder
195	  values and field explanations — e.g. `config/household.example.md`,
196	  `config/pantry-staples.example.md`, `config/store-rankings.example.md`.
197	- **Real files** (gitignored — personal data): the filled-in versions Claude
198	  reads/writes — e.g. `config/household.md`, etc.
199	- **Recipe collection:** structured markdown files, one per recipe. Each recipe's
200	  rating (1–5) and notes live in that file's frontmatter (5.7).
201	- **Grocery list output:** Apple Notes (not a project file).
202	
203	`.gitignore` must exclude all real personal-data files while keeping `.example`
204	templates.
205	
206	---
207	
208	## 8. Non-Functional Requirements
209	
210	- **Shareable & reproducible:** a new family clones the repo, copies `.example`
211	  templates to real filenames, completes kickoff, and runs. No personal data is
212	  ever committed.
213	- **Privacy:** addresses, names, ages, budget, and schedule stay in gitignored
214	  files / the user's own Apple account — never pushed to a public repo.
215	- **Low-friction editing:** config editable via files or natural-language chat
216	  with Claude.
217	
218	---
219	
220	## 9. Open Follow-ups
221	
222	- **Scheduled-job failure handling** — behavior when the calendar isn't filled in
223	  yet, or no one responds to a review/planning prompt (e.g. re-nudge vs. skip vs.
224	  wait for manual trigger).
225	- **Ratings frontmatter schema** — exact field names/format for the rating and
226	  notes in each recipe file (to be finalized during build).
227

# Requirements: Family Meal Planning Workflow

Derived from [[Project Plan]]. This document captures the MVP requirements for a
Claude-powered weekly dinner-planning workflow for a household, designed to be
backed up on GitHub and shared with other families.

---

## 1. Purpose & Problem

Family meal planning is a burdensome weekly task with many inputs and
dependencies that affects daily happiness. Done well it improves day-to-day life
with compounding effects on health and budget; done poorly it adds stress at the
end of a long day. Today the process is inconsistent week to week, rushed, and
cascades into either food waste (few shared ingredients) or overspending (one
convenient store).

**Goal:** a repeatable workflow that plans the week's dinners, produces a
store-grouped grocery list, and schedules prep reminders — so the family sticks
to dietary goals, shops smarter, and wastes less food.

## 2. Users

- **Primary:** the two partners in the household who alternate meal-planning duty
  each week.
- **Secondary:** other families who clone the shared GitHub project and run it
  for their own household after completing kickoff.

## 3. Success Criteria

- A full week of needed dinners is planned in a single sitting (no mid-week
  "rush and order out").
- Weekly planning time drops meaningfully below the current ~1 hr + ~15 min/day.
- Grocery list is organized so shopping is efficient and cost-aware.
- Prep reminders remove the daily "did we thaw the beef?" scramble.
- Another family can clone the repo, complete kickoff, and run their first week.

---

## 4. Scope

### 4.1 In Scope (MVP)

- One-time **kickoff** to capture household config.
- **Weekly run** (scheduled + manual) that plans dinners, builds the grocery
  list, and schedules prep reminders.
- **Hybrid recipe sourcing**: personal collection first, AI/web suggestions to
  fill gaps.
- **Store-grouped grocery list** using simple ranked-store logic.
- **Ratings** feedback loop collected twice weekly.
- **Shareable** project structure (templates + gitignored personal data).

### 4.2 Out of Scope (deferred to later versions)

- **Full grocery price optimization** — splitting a shopping trip across multiple
  stores based on live sale/circular pricing to minimize spend. MVP uses only
  simple ranked-store grouping (see 5.4). True price optimization requires sale
  data that stores do not expose via API.
- Breakfast/lunch planning (dinners only for MVP).
- Automated ordering / grocery delivery integration.
- Nutrition/macro tracking.

---

## 5. Functional Requirements

### 5.1 Kickoff (one-time per household)

Captured once and stored as editable config files (see 7). Collect:

1. Home address (used to derive nearby grocery stores).
2. Ranked list of grocery stores the family shops at.
3. Ideal/target dinner prep time.
4. Weekly budget.
5. Household members (names, ages).
6. Per-individual dietary preferences and requirements, including ingredients to
   avoid per person.
7. Ingredients typically on hand (spices, canned items, oils — pantry staples).
8. Cooking equipment available (stovetop, oven, air fryer, crockpot, etc.).
9. **Portion strategy** — how to size cooking when members are out on different
   nights (e.g. scale to who's home, plan deliberate leftovers, always
   full-family). This is a per-family preference, not fixed logic.
10. **Meal-planner run timing** — the family's preferred day/time for the weekly
    planning job (per-family; see 5.8).
11. Examples of up to 5 favorite dinner recipes (optional; seeds the collection).

### 5.2 Weekly Run

Triggered by the scheduled **meal-planner** job at the family's configured
day/time (set at kickoff; see 5.8), and also runnable **manually** at any time.
The job **pre-drafts** a plan and waits for the family's review/approval before
finalizing. Steps:

1. **Read the calendar first.** Pull the week's schedule from Apple Calendar to
   detect dinners-out (work dinners, dinner at a friend's, travel) and determine
   how many dinners are needed, and for whom, each night.
2. Confirm/adjust the number of dinners to plan for the week (configurable).
3. Select dinners using **hybrid sourcing** (5.3), respecting dietary
   requirements, prep-time target, budget, and available equipment.
4. Favor recipes with **shared ingredients** to reduce waste.
5. Assign meals to days based on who is home and prep constraints.
6. Generate the **store-grouped grocery list** (5.4), excluding pantry staples
   already on hand.
7. Schedule **prep reminders** (5.6).
8. Deliver outputs (5.5).

### 5.3 Recipe Sourcing (Hybrid)

- Draw first from the family's **personal recipe collection**.
- Fill remaining slots with **AI-generated and/or web-sourced** suggestions
  matched to the family's preferences and ratings (5.7).
- **Collection growth:** the user pastes a recipe link (NYT, TikTok, Instagram,
  blog) or a photo of a recipe card, and Claude extracts it into a structured
  markdown file in the project's recipe collection.

### 5.4 Grocery List (MVP)

- Aggregate all needed ingredients across the week's recipes.
- Exclude items already on hand (pantry staples from kickoff).
- **Group the list by store** using simple ranked-store logic — e.g. if multiple
  recipes need chicken, assign chicken to the preferred bulk store (Costco).
- No live pricing/sale lookups in MVP (see Out of Scope).

### 5.5 Outputs

1. **Menu for the week, by day.**
2. **Recipes + step-by-step instructions.**
3. **Grocery list grouped by store**, delivered to **Apple Notes** (shared, so
   both partners can access; can be uploaded back into Claude for the guided
   cooking UX with timers).
4. **Prep reminders** in Apple Reminders (5.6).

### 5.6 Prep Reminders

- Written to a dedicated Apple Reminders list named **"Family Meals"**.
- **Per-recipe timing** based on what each recipe needs (e.g. defrost = night
  before, marinate = morning of).

### 5.7 Ratings Feedback Loop

- **Scale:** 1–5, with optional free-text notes (e.g. "loved the bowl format,"
  "too spicy," "halve the garlic next time"). The notes carry the real signal.
- **One rating per recipe** — the current rating, overwritten when re-rated —
  stored in the recipe's own markdown file (frontmatter), so the rating travels
  with the recipe and stays personal-but-shareable.
- **Captured by the recipe-reviewer job** (5.8), which prompts the family to
  rate recently cooked meals.
- **Usage in planning:**
  - Boost highly-rated recipes for re-suggestion.
  - Suppress low-rated recipes.
  - Use ratings **and notes** to find *similar* highly-rated recipes — the notes
    reveal the pattern the family likes. (Example: a note that they loved the
    "in a bowl" format of a taco bowl signals suggesting more "X in a bowl"
    meals, not just that specific taco recipe.)

### 5.8 Scheduled Automation

Two **independent** scheduled jobs, kept separate so feedback is collected more
frequently and feeds the next week's planning. Both notify via **push
notification**.

1. **Recipe reviewer** — runs **twice weekly, Wednesday and Friday evenings**.
   Prompts the family to rate recently cooked meals (5.7). The higher review
   cadence keeps ratings fresh for the next planning run.
2. **Meal planner** — runs at the family's **preferred day/time (set at kickoff;
   5.1)**. **Pre-drafts** the week's plan from the calendar and preferences, then
   waits for the family's review/approval before finalizing. Also runnable
   manually (5.2).

---

## 6. Integrations & Platform

- **Claude Desktop** — build and run platform; user-facing interface. Claude's
  mobile app provides the guided recipe/cooking UX (timers) when a recipe note
  is uploaded.
- **apple-events MCP** (`FradSer/mcp-server-apple-events`, installed
  project-scoped via `.mcp.json`) — reads **Apple Calendar** (schedule) and
  writes **Apple Reminders** (the "Family Meals" prep list).
  - Note: this changes the original plan's "Google Calendar" — schedule lives in
    Apple Calendar (confirmed).
- **Apple Notes** — destination for the weekly grocery list and recipe handoff to
  mobile.
- **GitHub** — the workflow is backed up as a shareable project so other
  households can clone and run it.

---

## 7. Data & Storage Model

All persistent config and state live as **markdown files in the project** so they
can be edited directly or by chatting with Claude, and version-controlled.

- **Template files** (committed, shareable): `*.example.md` with placeholder
  values and field explanations — e.g. `config/household.example.md`,
  `config/pantry-staples.example.md`, `config/store-rankings.example.md`.
- **Real files** (gitignored — personal data): the filled-in versions Claude
  reads/writes — e.g. `config/household.md`, etc.
- **Recipe collection:** structured markdown files, one per recipe. Each recipe's
  rating (1–5) and notes live in that file's frontmatter (5.7).
- **Grocery list output:** Apple Notes (not a project file).

`.gitignore` must exclude all real personal-data files while keeping `.example`
templates.

---

## 8. Non-Functional Requirements

- **Shareable & reproducible:** a new family clones the repo, copies `.example`
  templates to real filenames, completes kickoff, and runs. No personal data is
  ever committed.
- **Privacy:** addresses, names, ages, budget, and schedule stay in gitignored
  files / the user's own Apple account — never pushed to a public repo.
- **Low-friction editing:** config editable via files or natural-language chat
  with Claude.

---

## 9. Open Follow-ups

- **Scheduled-job failure handling** — behavior when the calendar isn't filled in
  yet, or no one responds to a review/planning prompt (e.g. re-nudge vs. skip vs.
  wait for manual trigger).
- **Ratings frontmatter schema** — exact field names/format for the rating and
  notes in each recipe file (to be finalized during build).

file.content:
# Requirements: Family Meal Planning Workflow

Derived from [[Project Plan]]. This document captures the MVP requirements for a
Claude-powered weekly dinner-planning workflow for a household, designed to be
backed up on GitHub and shared with other families.

---

## 1. Purpose & Problem

Family meal planning is a burdensome weekly task with many inputs and
dependencies that affects daily happiness. Done well it improves day-to-day life
with compounding effects on health and budget; done poorly it adds stress at the
end of a long day. Today the process is inconsistent week to week, rushed, and
cascades into either food waste (few shared ingredients) or overspending (one
convenient store).

**Goal:** a repeatable workflow that plans the week's dinners, produces a
store-grouped grocery list, and schedules prep reminders — so the family sticks
to dietary goals, shops smarter, and wastes less food.

## 2. Users

- **Primary:** the two partners in the household who alternate meal-planning duty
  each week.
- **Secondary:** other families who clone the shared GitHub project and run it
  for their own household after completing kickoff.

## 3. Success Criteria

- A full week of needed dinners is planned in a single sitting (no mid-week
  "rush and order out").
- Weekly planning time drops meaningfully below the current ~1 hr + ~15 min/day.
- Grocery list is organized so shopping is efficient and cost-aware.
- Prep reminders remove the daily "did we thaw the beef?" scramble.
- Another family can clone the repo, complete kickoff, and run their first week.

---

## 4. Scope

### 4.1 In Scope (MVP)

- One-time **kickoff** to capture household config.
- **Weekly run** (scheduled + manual) that plans dinners, builds the grocery
  list, and schedules prep reminders.
- **Hybrid recipe sourcing**: personal collection first, AI/web suggestions to
  fill gaps.
- **Store-grouped grocery list** using simple ranked-store logic.
- **End-of-week ratings** feedback loop.
- **Shareable** project structure (templates + gitignored personal data).

### 4.2 Out of Scope (deferred to later versions)

- **Full grocery price optimization** — splitting a shopping trip across multiple
  stores based on live sale/circular pricing to minimize spend. MVP uses only
  simple ranked-store grouping (see 5.4). True price optimization requires sale
  data that stores do not expose via API.
- Breakfast/lunch planning (dinners only for MVP).
- Automated ordering / grocery delivery integration.
- Nutrition/macro tracking.

---

## 5. Functional Requirements

### 5.1 Kickoff (one-time per household)

Captured once and stored as editable config files (see 7). Collect:

1. Home address (used to derive nearby grocery stores).
2. Ranked list of grocery stores the family shops at.
3. Ideal/target dinner prep time.
4. Weekly budget.
5. Household members (names, ages).
6. Per-individual dietary preferences and requirements, including ingredients to
   avoid per person.
7. Ingredients typically on hand (spices, canned items, oils — pantry staples).
8. Cooking equipment available (stovetop, oven, air fryer, crockpot, etc.).
9. **Portion strategy** — how to size cooking when members are out on different
   nights (e.g. scale to who's home, plan deliberate leftovers, always
   full-family). This is a per-family preference, not fixed logic.
10. Examples of up to 5 favorite dinner recipes (optional; seeds the collection).

### 5.2 Weekly Run

Triggered by a **scheduled weekly routine** (e.g. Saturday morning) and also
runnable **manually** at any time. Steps:

1. **Read the calendar first.** Pull the week's schedule from Apple Calendar to
   detect dinners-out (work dinners, dinner at a friend's, travel) and determine
   how many dinners are needed, and for whom, each night.
2. Confirm/adjust the number of dinners to plan for the week (configurable).
3. Select dinners using **hybrid sourcing** (5.3), respecting dietary
   requirements, prep-time target, budget, and available equipment.
4. Favor recipes with **shared ingredients** to reduce waste.
5. Assign meals to days based on who is home and prep constraints.
6. Generate the **store-grouped grocery list** (5.4), excluding pantry staples
   already on hand.
7. Schedule **prep reminders** (5.6).
8. Deliver outputs (5.5).

### 5.3 Recipe Sourcing (Hybrid)

- Draw first from the family's **personal recipe collection**.
- Fill remaining slots with **AI-generated and/or web-sourced** suggestions
  matched to the family's preferences.
- **Collection growth:** the user pastes a recipe link (NYT, TikTok, Instagram,
  blog) or a photo of a recipe card, and Claude extracts it into a structured
  markdown file in the project's recipe collection.

### 5.4 Grocery List (MVP)

- Aggregate all needed ingredients across the week's recipes.
- Exclude items already on hand (pantry staples from kickoff).
- **Group the list by store** using simple ranked-store logic — e.g. if multiple
  recipes need chicken, assign chicken to the preferred bulk store (Costco).
- No live pricing/sale lookups in MVP (see Out of Scope).

### 5.5 Outputs

1. **Menu for the week, by day.**
2. **Recipes + step-by-step instructions.**
3. **Grocery list grouped by store**, delivered to **Apple Notes** (shared, so
   both partners can access; can be uploaded back into Claude for the guided
   cooking UX with timers).
4. **Prep reminders** in Apple Reminders (5.6).

### 5.6 Prep Reminders

- Written to a dedicated Apple Reminders list named **"Family Meals"**.
- **Per-recipe timing** based on what each recipe needs (e.g. defrost = night
  before, marinate = morning of).

### 5.7 Ratings Feedback Loop

- At the **end of each week**, as part of the scheduled routine, Claude prompts
  the family to rate the dinners they cooked.
- Ratings are logged to a persistent ratings file (see 7) and inform future
  recipe suggestions.

---

## 6. Integrations & Platform

- **Claude Desktop** — build and run platform; user-facing interface. Claude's
  mobile app provides the guided recipe/cooking UX (timers) when a recipe note
  is uploaded.
- **apple-events MCP** (`FradSer/mcp-server-apple-events`, installed
  project-scoped via `.mcp.json`) — reads **Apple Calendar** (schedule) and
  writes **Apple Reminders** (the "Family Meals" prep list).
  - Note: this changes the original plan's "Google Calendar" — schedule lives in
    Apple Calendar (confirmed).
- **Apple Notes** — destination for the weekly grocery list and recipe handoff to
  mobile.
- **GitHub** — the workflow is backed up as a shareable project so other
  households can clone and run it.

---

## 7. Data & Storage Model

All persistent config and state live as **markdown files in the project** so they
can be edited directly or by chatting with Claude, and version-controlled.

- **Template files** (committed, shareable): `*.example.md` with placeholder
  values and field explanations — e.g. `config/household.example.md`,
  `config/pantry-staples.example.md`, `config/store-rankings.example.md`.
- **Real files** (gitignored — personal data): the filled-in versions Claude
  reads/writes — e.g. `config/household.md`, etc.
- **Recipe collection:** structured markdown files, one per recipe.
- **Ratings:** a dedicated ratings file that grows over time.
- **Grocery list output:** Apple Notes (not a project file).

`.gitignore` must exclude all real personal-data files while keeping `.example`
templates.

---

## 8. Non-Functional Requirements

- **Shareable & reproducible:** a new family clones the repo, copies `.example`
  templates to real filenames, completes kickoff, and runs. No personal data is
  ever committed.
- **Privacy:** addresses, names, ages, budget, and schedule stay in gitignored
  files / the user's own Apple account — never pushed to a public repo.
- **Low-friction editing:** config editable via files or natural-language chat
  with Claude.

---

## 9. Open Follow-ups

- **Ratings schema** — scale (1–5?), fields (score, date, who, free-text notes),
  history vs. overwrite, storage location (per-recipe frontmatter vs. central
  file), and how ratings feed future suggestions.
- **Scheduled routine mechanics** — day/time for the planning kickoff and the
  end-of-week ratings prompt; autonomy level (nudge vs. pre-draft-and-wait);
  delivery channel; failure handling; and whether these are one or two jobs.

lastPrompt: yes requirements is part of it, the requirements-2.md is the one that was stuck in downloads

content.text:
Both files are readable now, and I've compared them. `requirements 2.md` (last edited 13:25) is clearly the newer, more complete version — the " 2" is just macOS's copy-collision suffix. Here's what it adds over the stale `requirements.md` (13:35... 12:35):

**Substantive upgrades in `requirements 2.md`:**
- **Ratings loop is now fully specified** (was an open question): 1–5 scale **+ free-text notes** ("the notes carry the real signal"), **one rating per recipe stored in that recipe's frontmatter** (not a central ratings file), and notes used to surface *similar* recipes (the "X in a bowl" pattern).
- **New §5.8 Scheduled Automation — two independent jobs:** a **recipe-reviewer** (twice weekly, Wed & Fri evenings) and a **meal-planner** (family's preferred day/time, pre-drafts then waits for approval). Both via push notification.
- **Kickoff §5.1** adds item #10 (meal-planner run timing); favorites recipe seed moves to #11.
- **§7 Data model** moves ratings into per-recipe frontmatter (drops the old standalone ratings file).
- **§9 Open follow-ups** shrinks — the old big unknowns (ratings schema, scheduled mechanics) are now resolved.

So `requirements 2.md` should become the canonical `requirements.md`, replacing the older one.

One caution before I touch anything: these `docs/` files are **not yet committed to git**, so overwriting the stale `requirements.md` is **not recoverable**. How do you want me to consolidate?

# Progress Notes — Family Cookbook Workflow

A running diary of steps taken while building out this workflow.

## 2026-07-09
- Added `/Users/****/Documents/mp-docs-synced/workflow-tools/family-cookbook` as a working directory (project-scoped, saved to `07_2026_family-cookbook/.claude/settings.local.json`).
- Created blank `requirements.md` in the mp-docs folder.
- Created this progress notes file to passively record build steps.
- Researched Claude Code options for Apple Reminders integration. Two mechanisms: MCP servers vs. skills; two bridges: AppleScript (slow) vs. EventKit (fast). Top candidates: `rem` (EventKit skill, fastest, Reminders-only), FradSer/mcp-server-apple-events (EventKit MCP, Reminders + Calendar), automating-mac-apps plugin (broad Apple-app automation). All run locally on macOS and need Reminders permission.
- Inspected the automating-mac-apps plugin: 16 skills (Reminders + Calendar/Mail/Notes/office suite/etc.), AppleScript-based, community repo. Reminders skill is self-contained. Concluded it's overkill for a Reminders-focused workflow.
- **Decision:** build on FradSer/mcp-server-apple-events (EventKit MCP, Reminders + Calendar). Verified: Node v25.3.0 (needs 20+), package v1.5.0 ships pre-built signed binary (no Xcode via npx). Tools: reminders_tasks, reminders_subtasks, reminders_lists, calendar_events, calendar_calendars.
- Installed it project-scoped via `07_2026_family-cookbook/.mcp.json` (server name `apple-events`). Pending: restart/approve the MCP server in Claude Code, then grant macOS Reminders/Calendar permission (prompts as "event") on first call.
- Interviewed on requirements (2 rounds) and wrote `requirements.md` from the Project Plan. Key decisions: hybrid recipe sourcing (personal MD collection via link/photo extraction + AI/web gap-fill); grocery list grouped by ranked stores (full price optimization deferred to out-of-scope); configurable dinner count with the weekly run starting by reading Apple Calendar for dinners-out; config stored as project markdown files + shareable `.example` templates (real files gitignored), grocery list output to Apple Notes; **Apple Calendar** via apple-events instead of Google Calendar; end-of-week ratings prompt as part of a scheduled routine; recipes handed to mobile via Apple Notes; per-recipe prep reminders in a dedicated "Family Meals" Reminders list; portion/scaling strategy captured as a per-family kickoff config; weekly run = scheduled routine + manual trigger.
- Moved deferred items (full price optimization, breakfast/lunch, auto-ordering, nutrition tracking) into the Project Plan's out-of-scope section.
- Open follow-ups logged in requirements.md: Google→Apple Calendar migration, ratings file schema, scheduled-routine mechanics.

# Progress Notes — Family Cookbook Workflow

A running diary of steps taken while building out this workflow.

## 2026-07-09
- Added `/Users/****/Documents/mp-docs-synced/workflow-tools/family-cookbook` as a working directory (project-scoped, saved to `07_2026_family-cookbook/.claude/settings.local.json`).
- Created blank `requirements.md` in the mp-docs folder.
- Created this progress notes file to passively record build steps.
- Researched Claude Code options for Apple Reminders integration. Two mechanisms: MCP servers vs. skills; two bridges: AppleScript (slow) vs. EventKit (fast). Top candidates: `rem` (EventKit skill, fastest, Reminders-only), FradSer/mcp-server-apple-events (EventKit MCP, Reminders + Calendar), automating-mac-apps plugin (broad Apple-app automation). All run locally on macOS and need Reminders permission.
- Inspected the automating-mac-apps plugin: 16 skills (Reminders + Calendar/Mail/Notes/office suite/etc.), AppleScript-based, community repo. Reminders skill is self-contained. Concluded it's overkill for a Reminders-focused workflow.
- **Decision:** build on FradSer/mcp-server-apple-events (EventKit MCP, Reminders + Calendar). Verified: Node v25.3.0 (needs 20+), package v1.5.0 ships pre-built signed binary (no Xcode via npx). Tools: reminders_tasks, reminders_subtasks, reminders_lists, calendar_events, calendar_calendars.
- Installed it project-scoped via `07_2026_family-cookbook/.mcp.json` (server name `apple-events`). Pending: restart/approve the MCP server in Claude Code, then grant macOS Reminders/Calendar permission (prompts as "event") on first call.
- Interviewed on requirements (2 rounds) and wrote `requirements.md` from the Project Plan. Key decisions: hybrid recipe sourcing (personal MD collection via link/photo extraction + AI/web gap-fill); grocery list grouped by ranked stores (full price optimization deferred to out-of-scope); configurable dinner count with the weekly run starting by reading Apple Calendar for dinners-out; config stored as project markdown files + shareable `.example` templates (real files gitignored), grocery list output to Apple Notes; **Apple Calendar** via apple-events instead of Google Calendar; end-of-week ratings prompt as part of a scheduled routine; recipes handed to mobile via Apple Notes; per-recipe prep reminders in a dedicated "Family Meals" Reminders list; portion/scaling strategy captured as a per-family kickoff config; weekly run = scheduled routine + manual trigger.
- Moved deferred items (full price optimization, breakfast/lunch, auto-ordering, nutrition tracking) into the Project Plan's out-of-scope section.
- Open follow-ups logged in requirements.md: Google→Apple Calendar migration, ratings file schema, scheduled-routine mechanics.

content.text: Good question — let me check what we've actually got in place versus what building requires. Let me read the Project Plan and the current scaffold state.

content.content:
1	# Project Plan: Dinner Planning for the week 
2	
3	The workflow today — Who does it, how often, and what makes it painful, slow, or repetitive? What does the current process look like step by step?
4	
5	## 1. Current workflow: 
6	
7	In our household, we trade off who does this each week, me or my partner. 
8		1. The biggest struggle of the workflow is its not consistent how its managed each week based on our schedules, and the day we do it. 
9		2. We try and do it every sunday to plan the rest of the week but then we often rush and only schedule through the first 3 days of the week and end up ordering out for one or two days at the end of the week. 
10		3. Right now we often optimize for speed and ease of getting the planning and shopping done, which is a cascading problem, if we are rushing the planning, that leaves us with a recipe list with few shared ingredients, which results in excess food at the end of the week that gets tossed. If we spend more time planning and get a good set of recipe's with shared ingredients, we'll often rush the shopping and try and go to one store that we can get as many things bought at once, which often means we're over paying for some of the ingredients. 
11		4. This problem is worth solving for our family and other familes as it currently takes up a significant amount of time each week minimum 1 hr planning, 15 mins each day planning for the next day to set reminders, confirm we got all the ingredients we need etc. And when we solve this problem effecitvely, we'll stick to our dietary goals better, we'll shop smarter (saving $) and waste less food. 
12	
13	1. Step by step current workflow:
14			1. First we add our plans for the week to our google calendar, so who ever is meal planning for the week, knows who's going to be home on which days for dinner. 
15			2. Meal planner for the week sits down on saturday or sunday, picks 3-4 dinners for the week from our recipe books and from things we've seen on ny times recipes, tiktok, instagram, etc. 
16			3. Next they check what ingredients are needed for each recipe, ones we already have at home like spices and oils are skipped, then the rest that we need to buy are added to a shared apple note for our grocery list for the week. 
17			4. Then they check the grocery store apps to see what ingredients are on sale for the week. 
18			5. Then they schedule out meals for different days of the week based on whos going to be home for dinner, and the order at which we're buying the ingredients. 
19	
20	
21	## AI Workflow Build Out:
22	
23	[[How to Write Problem Statements You’ll Actually Use]]
24	### Problem to Sovle: 
25	Family meal planning is a burdomsome weekly task that takes into account numorous inputs each week, has lots of dependencies and impacts our daily happiness. When done well, it can drastically improve our familys day to day life, and has componding effects on our health and wallet, when done incorrectly, it can cause excess stress at the end of a long day. 
26	
27	#### Factors to take into account weekly: 
28	1. Family Schedule for the week > Google Calendar MCP 
29	2. How many people are eating each night 
30	3. Cuisine preferences
31	4. Budget for the week
32	5. Ingredients typically on hand (spices, canned items, oils)
33	6. Cooking utensils at home, stove top, air fryer, crockpot, etc. 
34	7. Past ratings on individual recipes
35	
36	#### Output: 
37	1. Menu for the week by day
38	2. Recipie and instructions
39	3. Grocery list by store for the week
40	4. Reiminders scheduled for prep (take ground beef out of freezer etc)
41	
42	#### Individual inputs at kickoff: 
43	1. Your home address > used to pull list of grocery stores in the area
44	2. Your ranked grocery stores you shop at 
45	3. A family's Ideal prep time for dinners 
46	4. Weekly budget
47	5. Household to cook for (names, ages)
48	6. Individual Dietary Preferences and requirements. 
49		1. Ingredients to avoid based on each indivduals preferences. 
50	7. Ingredients typically on hand (spices, canned items, oils)
51	8. Cooking utensils at home, stove top, air fryer, crockpot, etc. 
52	9. Examples of your 5 favorite dinner recipes currently (optional)
53	
54	
55	### Building Process:
56	**What tools to use:**

60	- We'll build this on claude for desktop, need to make sure we have a way to send the recipes to claude on mobile devices for the cooking step. > backup plan apple notes integration
61	- End goal, the workflow needs to be backed up on a github project, so I can share it with friends and family members to use with their households. 
62

64	- **Full grocery price optimization** — splitting a shopping trip across multiple stores based on live sale/circular pricing to minimize spend. MVP uses only simple ranked-store grouping (e.g. buy chicken at Costco if multiple chicken recipes). True price optimization requires sale data stores don't expose via API.
65	- Breakfast/lunch planning (dinners only for MVP).
66	- Automated ordering / grocery delivery integration.
67	- Nutrition/macro tracking.

file.content:
# Project Plan: Dinner Planning for the week 

The workflow today — Who does it, how often, and what makes it painful, slow, or repetitive? What does the current process look like step by step?

## 1. Current workflow: 

In our household, we trade off who does this each week, me or my partner. 
	1. The biggest struggle of the workflow is its not consistent how its managed each week based on our schedules, and the day we do it. 
	2. We try and do it every sunday to plan the rest of the week but then we often rush and only schedule through the first 3 days of the week and end up ordering out for one or two days at the end of the week. 
	3. Right now we often optimize for speed and ease of getting the planning and shopping done, which is a cascading problem, if we are rushing the planning, that leaves us with a recipe list with few shared ingredients, which results in excess food at the end of the week that gets tossed. If we spend more time planning and get a good set of recipe's with shared ingredients, we'll often rush the shopping and try and go to one store that we can get as many things bought at once, which often means we're over paying for some of the ingredients. 
	4. This problem is worth solving for our family and other familes as it currently takes up a significant amount of time each week minimum 1 hr planning, 15 mins each day planning for the next day to set reminders, confirm we got all the ingredients we need etc. And when we solve this problem effecitvely, we'll stick to our dietary goals better, we'll shop smarter (saving $) and waste less food. 

1. Step by step current workflow:
		1. First we add our plans for the week to our google calendar, so who ever is meal planning for the week, knows who's going to be home on which days for dinner. 
		2. Meal planner for the week sits down on saturday or sunday, picks 3-4 dinners for the week from our recipe books and from things we've seen on ny times recipes, tiktok, instagram, etc. 
		3. Next they check what ingredients are needed for each recipe, ones we already have at home like spices and oils are skipped, then the rest that we need to buy are added to a shared apple note for our grocery list for the week. 
		4. Then they check the grocery store apps to see what ingredients are on sale for the week. 
		5. Then they schedule out meals for different days of the week based on whos going to be home for dinner, and the order at which we're buying the ingredients. 


## AI Workflow Build Out:

[[How to Write Problem Statements You’ll Actually Use]]
### Problem to Sovle: 
Family meal planning is a burdomsome weekly task that takes into account numorous inputs each week, has lots of dependencies and impacts our daily happiness. When done well, it can drastically improve our familys day to day life, and has componding effects on our health and wallet, when done incorrectly, it can cause excess stress at the end of a long day. 

#### Factors to take into account weekly: 
1. Family Schedule for the week > Google Calendar MCP 
2. How many people are eating each night 
3. Cuisine preferences
4. Budget for the week
5. Ingredients typically on hand (spices, canned items, oils)
6. Cooking utensils at home, stove top, air fryer, crockpot, etc. 
7. Past ratings on individual recipes

#### Output: 
1. Menu for the week by day
2. Recipie and instructions
3. Grocery list by store for the week
4. Reiminders scheduled for prep (take ground beef out of freezer etc)

#### Individual inputs at kickoff: 
1. Your home address > used to pull list of grocery stores in the area
2. Your ranked grocery stores you shop at 
3. A family's Ideal prep time for dinners 
4. Weekly budget
5. Household to cook for (names, ages)
6. Individual Dietary Preferences and requirements. 
	1. Ingredients to avoid based on each indivduals preferences. 
7. Ingredients typically on hand (spices, canned items, oils)
8. Cooking utensils at home, stove top, air fryer, crockpot, etc. 
9. Examples of your 5 favorite dinner recipes currently (optional)


### Building Process:
**What tools to use:** 
- Claude for the user facing UI, it has a great "recipe" UX on mobile that walks you through the steps for cooking and has built in timers. 
- Google Calendar > current schedule for the week, no additional changes in process 
- Apple Reminders > integreate with current workflow, no additional changes in process > use claude to find MCP for this
- We'll build this on claude for desktop, need to make sure we have a way to send the recipes to claude on mobile devices for the cooking step. > backup plan apple notes integration
- End goal, the workflow needs to be backed up on a github project, so I can share it with friends and family members to use with their households. 

*Ideas but out of scope for this intial MVP:*
- **Full grocery price optimization** — splitting a shopping trip across multiple stores based on live sale/circular pricing to minimize spend. MVP uses only simple ranked-store grouping (e.g. buy chicken at Costco if multiple chicken recipes). True price optimization requires sale data stores don't expose via API.
- Breakfast/lunch planning (dinners only for MVP).
- Automated ordering / grocery delivery integration.
- Nutrition/macro tracking.

content.text:
Short answer: **yes — the requirements are complete enough to start building.** But it's worth being precise about what "building" means here, because it changes what we do next.

## What this actually is
This isn't a coded app (no React/server). It's a **Claude Code workflow** made of:
- **Config files** — `config/*.example.md` templates + gitignored real versions (household, pantry, store rankings)
- **A recipe collection** — one markdown file per recipe, rating in frontmatter
- **Skills / slash-commands** — kickoff, weekly meal-planner, recipe-reviewer, recipe-import
- **MCP wiring** — `apple-events` for Calendar (read) + Reminders (write); Apple Notes for grocery-list output
- **Two scheduled jobs** — meal-planner + recipe-reviewer, with push notifications

The Project Plan's "run on Claude Desktop" reconciles cleanly with this: **Claude Code is the build + orchestration home** (skills, MCP, scheduling, push), and the **mobile cooking UX is reached via the Apple Notes handoff** that §5.5 already specifies. No conflict there.

## Where we stand
**Done:** requirements ✅, project plan ✅, `.mcp.json` (apple-events) configured, MCP trusted in settings.

**Not built yet:** everything in the bullet list above — no `config/`, no `recipes/`, no skills, no scheduled jobs, and `.gitignore` doesn't yet exclude the real personal config/recipe files (only the docs).

## The one real prerequisite before building on top
The whole weekly run depends on the **apple-events MCP actually working** — connecting, and getting macOS Calendar/Reminders permission (it prompts on first call). I'd verify that *before* writing skills against it, so we're not building on an unverified integration. I can run a quick read-Calendar / write-a-test-Reminder check now — it will trigger macOS permission prompts, so I'll only do it if you say go.

## Proposed build order
1. **Foundation** (platform-independent, safe to start immediately): `config/*.example.md` templates, recipe file + frontmatter schema, `recipes/` structure, and `.gitignore` rules for personal data.
2. **Kickoff** skill — interviews you, writes the real config files.
3. **Weekly meal-planner** — calendar → plan (shared-ingredient logic) → store-grouped grocery list to Apple Notes → prep reminders.
4. **Recipe-reviewer** (ratings capture) + **recipe-import** (link/photo → structured markdown).
5. **Scheduled automation** — the two jobs + push notifications.
6. **Share polish** — README, example data, "clone and run" instructions.

Two small open items from requirements §9 (scheduled-job failure handling, exact ratings frontmatter schema) we can settle as we hit them — I'll propose defaults rather than block on them.

**My recommendation:** let me verify the MCP first, then start on Phase 1 (the foundation). Want me to kick off the MCP check?

questions.question: When the scheduled meal-planner runs but your Apple Calendar isn't filled in for the week (so it can't tell who's home or which nights are dinners-out), what should it do?

options.label: Draft anyway w/ defaults
options.description: Use the configured default dinner count and assume the full family is home every night, draft the plan, and clearly flag those assumptions in the output so you can adjust. Keeps momentum.
options.preview:
Meal-planner runs → calendar empty
  → assume: 5 dinners (from config), full family home
  → draft plan + grocery list
  → output header: ⚠️ "Calendar was empty — assumed
     full family home all week. Edit & re-run if wrong."

options.label: Push a reminder first
options.description: Send a push notification asking you to fill in the calendar (or confirm who's home), and wait for your reply before drafting anything.
options.preview:
Meal-planner runs → calendar empty
  → push: "Fill in this week's calendar so I can
     plan dinners — reply when ready."
  → wait (no draft yet)

options.label: Skip until manual run
options.description: Do nothing this cycle, send a notification that planning was skipped because the calendar was empty, and rely on you triggering it manually.
options.preview:
Meal-planner runs → calendar empty
  → push: "Skipped weekly planning — calendar
     was empty. Run manually when ready."
  → no draft, no list

questions.question: When the meal-planner has pre-drafted a plan but you don't respond to the approval prompt, what should happen? (For the ratings-review job, no response just means no rating that cycle — harmless.)

options.label: Re-nudge once, then wait
options.description: Send one reminder a few hours later. If still no response, the draft sits ready for manual approval — it never auto-creates the grocery list or reminders without a human OK.
options.preview:
Draft ready → no approval
  → +4h: push reminder (once)
  → still nothing → draft held for manual approval
  → NO auto grocery list / reminders

options.label: Auto-finalize at deadline
options.description: If still unapproved by a deadline (e.g. the evening before your shopping day), finalize the draft as-is so the grocery list and prep reminders still get created.
options.preview:
Draft ready → no approval
  → deadline (eve before shop day) reached
  → finalize draft as-is
  → grocery list → Apple Notes
  → prep reminders → Apple Reminders

options.label: Skip this week
options.description: Abandon the draft entirely. No grocery list or reminders are created unless you trigger a run manually.
options.preview:
Draft ready → no approval
  → discard draft
  → no list, no reminders
  → wait for manual trigger

questions.question: Which ratings frontmatter schema should each recipe file use? (These fields sit alongside the recipe's other frontmatter like title, source, servings, prep_time.)

options.label: Minimal
options.description: Current rating (1–5, overwritten on re-rate), free-text notes, and the last-cooked date. Matches the requirements as written — notes carry the signal for finding similar recipes.
options.preview:
---
title: Chicken Taco Bowls
# ...other recipe fields...
rating: 4          # 1–5, current only
rating_notes: "loved the bowl format;
  halve the garlic next time"
last_cooked: 2026-07-08
---

options.label: Minimal + cook count
options.description: Same as Minimal, plus a times_cooked counter that increments each time the recipe is used — a cheap signal of household favorites beyond the star rating.
options.preview:
---
title: Chicken Taco Bowls
# ...other recipe fields...
rating: 4
rating_notes: "loved the bowl format;
  halve the garlic next time"
last_cooked: 2026-07-08
times_cooked: 3
---

options.label: + structured traits
options.description: Adds a liked_traits tag list (e.g. bowl-format, one-pan, quick) on top of free-text notes, giving the planner structured hooks for 'find similar' matching — slightly more to maintain.
options.preview:
---
title: Chicken Taco Bowls
# ...other recipe fields...
rating: 4
rating_notes: "loved the bowl format"
liked_traits: [bowl-format, one-pan, quick]
last_cooked: 2026-07-08
times_cooked: 3
---

content.content:
65	## 5. Functional Requirements
66	
67	### 5.1 Kickoff (one-time per household)
68	
69	Captured once and stored as editable config files (see 7). Collect:
70	
71	1. Home address (used to derive nearby grocery stores).
72	2. Ranked list of grocery stores the family shops at.
73	3. Ideal/target dinner prep time.
74	4. Weekly budget.
75	5. Household members (names, ages).
76	6. Per-individual dietary preferences and requirements, including ingredients to
77	   avoid per person.
78	7. Ingredients typically on hand (spices, canned items, oils — pantry staples).
79	8. Cooking equipment available (stovetop, oven, air fryer, crockpot, etc.).
80	9. **Portion strategy** — how to size cooking when members are out on different
81	   nights (e.g. scale to who's home, plan deliberate leftovers, always
82	   full-family). This is a per-family preference, not fixed logic.
83	10. **Meal-planner run timing** — the family's preferred day/time for the weekly
84	    planning job (per-family; see 5.8).
85	11. Examples of up to 5 favorite dinner recipes (optional; seeds the collection).
86	
87	### 5.2 Weekly Run
88	
89	Triggered by the scheduled **meal-planner** job at the family's configured
90	day/time (set at kickoff; see 5.8), and also runnable **manually** at any time.
91	The job **pre-drafts** a plan and waits for the family's review/approval before
